title: The New City
bullets: Around 814 BCE, a Tyrian princess named Elissa (later called Dido by Latin writers) fled her brother Pygmalion's murder of her husband and founded Qart Hadasht, the New City, on the coast of modern Tunisia.
Almost nothing we know about Carthage comes from the Carthaginians themselves, because when Rome destroyed the city in 146 BCE it destroyed or dispersed virtually the entire written record.
Carthage sat near the narrowest point of the central Mediterranean, where the Tunisian coast and Sicily almost pinch the sea shut, controlling the flow of trade like a highway toll booth.
The young colony developed trade networks independent of Tyre as its mother city fell under Neo-Assyrian pressure, and by roughly 700 BCE Carthage was the dominant Phoenician city in the west.
The chief deities became Baal Hammon, a bearded elder with ram's horns, and Tanit, a lunar goddess whose distinctive triangle-circle-bar symbol appears on thousands of Punic artifacts.

The Tophet, a sacred precinct established around 750 BCE, accumulated thousands of urns containing cremated infant remains over six centuries, fueling the fiercest debate in Punic archaeology: child sacrifice or sacred burial ground.
Carthaginian scribes developed the Punic script, a cursive variant of the Phoenician alphabet, and Neo-Punic inscriptions appear in North Africa as late as the third or fourth century CE.

By 600 BCE, Carthage had ringed its peninsula with triple fortification walls stretching over 20 miles, reportedly 45 feet high and 30 feet thick, with stables for 300 war elephants built into the walls.

title: Masters of the Western Sea
bullets: Around 580 BCE, General Mago I seized power and established the Magonid dynasty, transforming Carthage from a wealthy trading city into an aggressive military power contesting Greek dominance across the western Mediterranean.
At the Battle of Alalia around 535 BCE, a combined Carthaginian-Etruscan fleet of 120 ships drove Phocaean Greek colonists from Corsica, dividing the western Mediterranean into spheres of influence.

Hanno the Navigator led sixty ships down the West African coast around 500 BCE, possibly reaching modern Cameroon, observing creatures his interpreters called Gorillae, the name Western scientists gave gorillas twenty-three centuries later.

Himilco sailed north through the Strait of Gibraltar to the tin-producing regions of Brittany or Britain, establishing Carthage's Atlantic trade network, routes so valuable that one captain wrecked his own ship rather than let a Roman vessel follow.
In 509 BCE, Carthage and the young Roman Republic signed their first commercial treaty, with Carthage treating Rome as a minor regional power controlling roughly 300 square miles of Italian farmland.
Hamilcar I, the last great Magonid ruler, spent his reign expanding Carthaginian territory in Sicily and Sardinia, building the military machine that would be tested at Himera in 480 BCE.

title: The African Empire
bullets: At the Battle of Himera in 480 BCE, the same year as the Greek victory at Salamis, Gelon of Syracuse routed Hamilcar I's invasion force and killed Hamilcar, knocking Carthage out of Sicilian military politics for seventy years.

Carthage reformed its government around two annually elected suffetes, a Council of Elders, and a popular assembly, a mixed constitution that Aristotle ranked alongside Sparta and Crete as one of the best-governed in the Mediterranean.
Mago the agricultural writer produced a 28-volume treatise on farming around 450 BCE, the only Carthaginian book Rome deliberately saved when it destroyed the city's libraries.
Carthaginian merchants conducted silent trade with West African peoples beyond the Sahara, exchanging Mediterranean goods for gold dust without either party speaking the other's language.

In 409 BCE, Hannibal Mago, grandson of the Hamilcar killed at Himera, sacked and razed the city in a vendetta seventy years in the making, reportedly executing 3,000 Greek prisoners on the spot where his grandfather fell.
At the Battle of the Crimissus in 341 BCE, the Corinthian general Timoleon killed 10,000 members of the Sacred Band, the worst single-day loss of citizen soldiers in Carthaginian history.

The Cothon, Carthage's double harbor completed around 400 BCE, sheltered 220 warships in individual covered berths ringing a central admiralty island, the most sophisticated naval installation in the ancient world.

Kerkouane, the only intact Punic city ever found, was abandoned in the mid-third century BCE and never rebuilt, preserving gridded streets, indoor bathing rooms, mosaic floors, and an industrial quarter for purple dye production.

title: Rome
bullets: The First Punic War began in 264 BCE over a minor dispute in Messana and lasted 23 years, the longest continuous military conflict in ancient history to that point, permanently altering the Mediterranean balance of power.
Rome besieged Agrigentum for seven months in 262 BCE, selling 25,000 inhabitants into slavery, and learned that controlling Sicilian cities was pointless while Carthage could resupply them by sea.

Rome built a fleet from scratch using a captured Carthaginian quinquereme as a model, then added the corvus, a boarding bridge that turned naval battles into infantry fights and neutralized Carthaginian seamanship.
At the Battle of Cape Ecnomus in 256 BCE, one of the largest naval engagements in ancient history with over 600 ships, Rome's victory opened the sea route to Africa.
Hamilcar Barca seized Mount Eryx in 247 BCE and waged a brilliant six-year guerrilla campaign against Rome, never defeated in the field, earning the surname Barca, meaning lightning.
After the war, Carthage's unpaid mercenaries revolted in the Truceless War, nearly destroying the state in a savage three-year conflict that Polybius called the most brutal he had ever recorded.

While Carthage fought its own mercenaries, Rome seized Sardinia and Corsica in 238 BCE in what even the pro-Roman historian Polybius called an act of naked opportunism with no legitimate justification.

title: The Barcid Project
bullets: In 237 BCE, Hamilcar Barca crossed to Iberia with his nine-year-old son Hannibal, who had sworn an oath at the Temple of Baal Hammon: eternal enmity against Rome.

Hamilcar spent nine years conquering southern and eastern Iberia, seizing silver mines with the systematic focus of a mining executive to fund the army, pay off Roman indemnities, and prepare for a second war.
Hasdrubal the Fair founded Carthago Nova, modern Cartagena, beside the richest silver mines in the western Mediterranean, which reportedly yielded 300 pounds of silver daily employing 40,000 workers.
The Ebro Treaty established the Ebro River as the boundary between Roman and Carthaginian spheres of influence, a line designed to prevent war that worked only as long as both sides respected it.

After Hasdrubal's assassination in 221 BCE, the army acclaimed twenty-five-year-old Hannibal Barca as commander, and even Roman writers acknowledged him as the finest tactician the Mediterranean world had produced.

Hannibal besieged and destroyed Saguntum in 219 BCE, deliberately exploiting the ambiguity of a Roman-allied city inside the Carthaginian sphere, provoking the war he had been planning since childhood.
Hannibal's plan was to march overland from Spain, across southern Gaul, over the Alps, and into the heart of Roman territory with 50,000 infantry, 9,000 cavalry, and 37 war elephants.

title: The War That Changed Everything
bullets: Hannibal crossed the Alps in fifteen days in late October 218 BCE, losing nearly half his army to rockslides, snowstorms, and hostile mountain tribes, arriving in northern Italy with roughly 26,000 infantry and a handful of surviving elephants.

At the Battle of Lake Trasimene in 217 BCE, Hannibal executed the largest successful ambush in military history, killing or capturing 30,000 Romans including the consul Flaminius in a single morning fog.

The Battle of Cannae in 216 BCE is the most studied tactical engagement in military history: Hannibal's double envelopment annihilated roughly 50,000 to 70,000 Roman soldiers, including 80 senators, in a single afternoon.

Rome refused to negotiate after Cannae, forbade even the discussion of peace terms, raised new armies, and adopted the Fabian strategy of avoiding pitched battles while isolating Hannibal in southern Italy.

Hasdrubal Barca crossed the Alps with reinforcements in 207 BCE but was intercepted and killed at the Battle of the Metaurus, his severed head catapulted into Hannibal's camp, reportedly the first time Hannibal wept.

Scipio Africanus invaded North Africa in 204 BCE, forcing Carthage to recall Hannibal after fifteen years of undefeated campaigning in Italy, then defeated him at the Battle of Zama using his own double-envelopment technique.
The peace terms ended Carthage as a great power: no fleet except ten ships, no overseas territories, 10,000 talents of silver over fifty years, and no war without Roman permission.
Hannibal served briefly as suffete, reforming finances, but Roman pressure drove him into exile in 195 BCE, and he took poison in Bithynia in 183 BCE rather than be captured.

title: How the Carthaginians Lived
bullets: By the fifth century BCE, Tanit had eclipsed Baal Hammon as Carthage's most prominent deity, her name appearing first on Tophet inscriptions: To the Lady Tanit, Face of Baal, and to the Lord Baal Hammon.

The Tophet debate remains genuinely unresolved: thousands of urns with cremated infant remains, votive stelae with dedicatory texts, and both sides of the argument rest on three strong pillars of evidence.

Carthage's economy combined purple dye production, agricultural exports from African estates, trans-Saharan gold trade, Atlantic tin trade, and coinage featuring the head of Tanit and a horse, struck from around 410 BCE.

Punic sailors navigated by Ursa Minor, maintained classified rutters covering the Atlantic coast, and may have reached the Azores, 900 miles into the open Atlantic, based on coin finds of disputed provenance.

Carthage relied on mercenaries and allied contingents rather than citizen soldiers: Libyan infantry, Numidian cavalry who rode without saddles or bridles, Iberian swordsmen, and Balearic slingers accurate at 100 yards.

North African forest elephants, a now-extinct subspecies smaller than Indian elephants, were Carthage's most iconic military asset, devastating when everything went right but as dangerous to friendly troops when panicked.

Terence, a Carthaginian-born slave freed by a Roman senator, became one of Rome's greatest comic playwrights, his line 'I am human, and I think nothing human is alien to me' among the most quoted sentences in Western literature.

title: Carthago Delenda Est
bullets: Carthage's economic recovery was so rapid that by the 190s BCE the city offered to pay off its entire fifty-year debt ahead of schedule, an act of commercial resilience that terrified the Roman Senate.
Cato the Elder ended every speech in the Senate with Carthago delenda est, Carthage must be destroyed, brandishing fresh Carthaginian figs to show how close and prosperous the old enemy remained.

When Rome demanded Carthage resettle 10 miles inland, the disarmed city refused, and the entire population mobilized: workshops became weapons factories, citizens melted household metal for swords, women cut their hair for bowstrings.

Scipio Aemilianus, the adopted grandson of Scipio Africanus, sealed the harbor, cut supply lines, and breached the walls in 146 BCE, beginning six days of house-by-house urban combat up the streets to Byrsa Hill.

The last defenders burned alive in the Temple of Eshmun rather than surrender, the commander's wife cursing her husband who had offered himself to Scipio's mercy before killing her children and throwing herself into the flames.

Rome razed the city, enslaved 50,000 survivors, and distributed Carthage's libraries to Numidian princes, saving only Mago's agricultural treatise and consigning seven centuries of Punic literature to oblivion.
In the same year 146 BCE, Rome also destroyed Corinth, the twin destructions conventionally marking the moment Rome became undisputed master of the entire Mediterranean.

The Punic language survived in rural North Africa for centuries after the destruction, Saint Augustine noting his parishioners still spoke it in the early fifth century CE, but the civilization that built the Cothon and produced Hannibal was finished.
